questions.question: Which statement best describes modern slavery?
options: A historical crime that no longer happens in the UK today.
The exploitation and control of a person for someone else's gain, so that they cannot leave freely.
A problem that only affects people who have entered the country illegally.
A situation people can always simply walk away from whenever they choose.

questions.question: Where does modern slavery happen in England?
options: Only overseas, never within the UK.
Only in large cities.
Only in places where crimes are obviously being committed in public.
In cities, towns and rural areas, often deliberately hidden from view.

questions.question: What is the main law dealing with modern slavery in England and Wales?
options: The Human Rights Act 1998.
The Care Act 2014.
The Modern Slavery Act 2015.
The Equality Act 2010.

questions.question: Which offences did the Modern Slavery Act 2015 bring together and strengthen?
options: Theft, burglary and fraud.
Slavery, servitude, forced or compulsory labour, and human trafficking.
Speeding, drink-driving and other road offences.
Fly-tipping and other environmental offences.

questions.question: As a care worker, how should you view modern slavery?
options: As both a serious crime and a safeguarding issue you should report like any other safeguarding concern.
As purely a police matter that has nothing to do with your role.
As something you must fully investigate yourself before acting.
As only a housing or employment problem.

questions.question: Preventing abuse and exploitation sits at the heart of safeguarding duties under which Act?
options: The Care Act 2014.
The Modern Slavery Act 2015.
The Data Protection Act 2018.
The Mental Capacity Act 2005.

questions.question: In forced or compulsory labour, how is a person typically made to work against their will?
options: Through violence, threats, deception or debt.
Through their own free choice to take on extra shifts.
Through generous pay and flexible hours.
Through a fair written contract they freely signed.

questions.question: What is a common way people are kept trapped in forced labour?
options: They are given their passport back and allowed to leave at any time.
They are told they owe a large debt for travel, food or rent that they can never repay.
They are offered training and promotion opportunities.
They are paid in full and helped to find better work.

questions.question: What is sexual exploitation?
options: Any consensual relationship between two adults.
When a person is forced or coerced into sexual activity for someone else's profit.
Something that only ever affects adults, never children.
A private matter that is never linked to modern slavery.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes domestic servitude?
options: A well-paid live-in job with regular time off and freedom to come and go.
Occasional help with housework given willingly between friends.
Someone forced to work in a private household for little or no pay, with almost no freedom.
A short work placement with a written contract and fair wages.

questions.question: What does the term 'county lines' refer to?
options: Gangs using children and vulnerable adults to move and sell drugs from cities into smaller towns and rural areas.
The routes used by lawful delivery companies between counties.
The boundaries used to organise local authority safeguarding teams.
A telephone advice service for reporting modern slavery.

questions.question: In criminal exploitation, what happens to the victim relative to those controlling them?
options: The victim shares the profits equally with the gang.
The victim is protected from any risk or punishment.
The victim carries the risk and any punishment while others take the profit.
The victim is always treated leniently because they are clearly a victim.

questions.question: Someone you support suddenly has unexplained cash and a new phone, goes missing for days, and seems fearful of an older 'friend'. What should you do?
options: Assume they are simply choosing to break the law and say nothing.
Wait several weeks to see whether the behaviour continues before mentioning it.
Confront the older 'friend' yourself to find out what is going on.
Treat it as possible criminal exploitation and report your concern to your manager or safeguarding lead.

questions.question: What defines human trafficking?
options: That the person is always taken across an international border.
That money always changes hands between the victim and the trafficker.
That a person is moved or held for the purpose of exploitation.
That the person has agreed in writing to be moved.

questions.question: Which statement about human trafficking is correct?
options: It only involves labour, never sexual exploitation or crime.
It can only happen when someone crosses a national border.
It can happen within the UK, and even within a single town.
It never happens in the UK.

questions.question: What is debt bondage?
options: A victim being told they owe a huge sum, with impossible interest added, so the debt can never be cleared.
A one-off gift of money that never has to be repaid.
A normal bank loan repaid in fair monthly instalments.
A savings scheme that helps a worker put money aside.

questions.question: Which of these is most likely to suggest that someone could be a victim of modern slavery?
options: A person who talks openly and freely about their home and their work.
Several signs together, such as fearfulness, untreated injuries, rehearsed answers and reluctance to give information.
A single bruise in a person who has clearly explained a recent accidental fall.
Someone simply having a quiet or tired day but otherwise settled.

questions.question: Which of the following is a clear sign that another person may be controlling a possible victim?
options: The person freely attends their own GP appointments alone.
The person comes and goes from home as they please.
The person keeps and manages their own money.
Someone accompanies them everywhere and answers all questions on their behalf.

questions.question: Which of these, relating to documents, is a warning sign of modern slavery?
options: The person keeps their own passport in a safe place.
The person can easily prove their identity when asked.
The person cannot produce their own documents because a passport or ID has been taken and is held by someone else.
The person has recently renewed their own driving licence.

questions.question: Which group of people is more likely to be targeted for modern slavery?
options: People who are socially well connected and financially secure.
People who are isolated, in poverty or debt, or who have insecure immigration status or care and support needs.
Only people who live alone in rural areas.
Only people who have committed crimes in the past.

questions.question: If you suspect modern slavery, what is your first duty?
options: Removing the person from the situation immediately by yourself.
Making the exploiter aware that you are suspicious.
Gathering enough evidence to prove the crime.
Safety, both yours and the person's.

questions.question: Why should you not confront a suspected trafficker or controller?
options: Because it usually resolves the situation quickly and safely.
Because it can put the victim in greater danger, tip off the exploiter, and expose you to harm.
Because it is against your organisation's dress code.
Because the trafficker will always simply admit what they are doing.

questions.question: If anyone is in immediate danger, what should you do?
options: Treat it as an emergency and call 999.
Post about it on social media to warn others.
Wait until your next supervision to raise it.
Confront the person you suspect is responsible.

questions.question: Within your organisation, who should you normally report a modern slavery concern to first?
options: A member of the person's family you have never met.
Nobody, until you are completely certain.
Your manager or safeguarding lead.
The suspected trafficker.

questions.question: What is the National Referral Mechanism (NRM)?
options: A register of everyone convicted of modern slavery offences.
A telephone helpline that replaces calling 999.
A charity that employs care workers as investigators.
The UK's official system for identifying and supporting victims of modern slavery.

questions.question: Modern slavery is both a serious crime and a safeguarding issue that you should report like any other safeguarding concern.
options: True
False

questions.question: Modern slavery is a historical crime that no longer happens in the UK today.
options: True
False

questions.question: Victims of modern slavery are always foreign nationals; British people are never affected.
options: True
False

questions.question: You do not need to be certain that modern slavery is happening before you report your concern.
options: True
False

questions.question: It can be a warning sign of modern slavery when someone else insists on speaking for a person and holds their identity documents.
options: True
False

questions.question: Human trafficking always involves a person being taken across an international border.
options: True
False

questions.question: If you suspect modern slavery, you should confront the suspected trafficker directly to get answers.
options: True
False

questions.question: If someone is in immediate danger, you should treat it as an emergency and call 999.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are recognised types of modern slavery covered in this course? (Select all that apply)
options: A fairly paid job with a genuine written contract
Domestic servitude
Forced or compulsory labour
Sexual exploitation
Freely choosing to change your employer

questions.question: Which of the following are warning signs that someone may be in forced labour? (Select all that apply)
options: They have little freedom to leave
They are paid fairly and have a genuine contract
Their identity documents are held by someone else
Their wages appear to be withheld or taken from them
They keep their own passport and come and go as they please

questions.question: Which of these can be signs that another person is controlling a possible victim? (Select all that apply)
options: The person is rarely, if ever, left unaccompanied
The person freely attends appointments alone
Someone else always answers questions on the person's behalf
Another person holds their money and documents
The person manages their own bank account and money

questions.question: Which of the following put a person at greater risk of modern slavery? (Select all that apply)
options: A learning disability, mental ill health or care and support needs
Insecure immigration status
A strong support network and secure finances
Isolation, poverty or debt

questions.question: When responding to suspected modern slavery, what should you do? (Select all that apply)
options: Promise the person you will keep a dangerous secret
Report your concern to your manager or safeguarding lead
Take photographs of the person and their accommodation as evidence
Avoid confronting the suspected trafficker or controller
Keep yourself and the person safe

questions.question: Which of these are appropriate ways to raise a modern slavery concern? (Select all that apply)
options: Post about your concern on social media
Warn the suspected trafficker directly
Contact the Modern Slavery Helpline for advice
Report it to your manager or safeguarding lead
Call 999 in an emergency

questions.question: When recording a modern slavery concern, your record should be: (Select all that apply)
options: Shared publicly so that others can be warned
Specific, including dates, times, places and exact words where possible
Stored and shared securely as your organisation's policy requires
Factual, describing what you observed rather than what you assume
Based mainly on your personal opinions and conclusions

questions.question: If someone is in immediate danger, always call ___.
The main law dealing with modern slavery in England and Wales is the Modern Slavery Act ___.
A powerful tool of control, where a victim is told they owe a huge sum that can never be repaid, is called ___ bondage.
The UK's official system for identifying and supporting victims of modern slavery is the National ___ Mechanism.
When you have a concern, your first port of call within your organisation is your manager or ___ lead.
